title: What Is the Mystery Behind This Sudden Settlement with the Income Tax Department?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. The Tamil reading copy below restores the source-printed opening salutation and corrects clear OCR character and word-boundary errors encountered during translation; the canonical source document remains unchanged. Legal allegations, representations, and judicial observations are kept explicitly attributed to the letter's speakers and cited proceedings. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The Income Tax Department's case against Tamil Nadu Chief Minister Jayalalithaa and her companion whom she called her sister, Sasikala, has continued for eighteen years. Just as it was approaching its conclusion, Judge Dakshinamurthy firmly directed that Jayalalithaa appear in court. A fresh petition filed on her behalf stated that they had applied to the Department to resolve the matter administratively and that the application was pending. Since counsel for the Income Tax Department raised no objection, the judge adjourned the hearing to 24 July. I discussed this in detail on 3 July 2014.

This case has remained pending for eighteen years before the First Economic Offences Court in the Egmore court complex. After the courts, the government and lawyers have spent countless hours over eighteen years, why did Jayalalithaa's side not take this same decision eighteen years ago and attempt to resolve the issue through the Department then? The case went all the way to the Supreme Court and consumed its precious time. Could they not have said at that stage that they wished to settle the matter through the Department?
Had they already submitted an application to the Department seeking an administrative settlement? If so, what happened to it? What decision was taken then?

In a letter concerning this matter to the President and Prime Minister, DMDK leader Vijayakant wrote that, during the eighteen years of the case, Jayalalithaa had not appeared before the court even once despite repeated judicial directions. He asked whether an ordinary Indian citizen could have acted in this manner when the judge had ordered Jayalalithaa to appear on 30 June 2014, and whether the law and courts would have permitted it.

He further questioned whether it was proper for Jayalalithaa now to inform the Income Tax Department's Director General that she was ready to pay the tax arrears and settle the matter through compounding. He said that the public believed acceptance of her request by the Department could divert the course of the disproportionate-assets case pending against her in the Bengaluru Special Court, and urged the Department to bear this in mind when deciding on the proposed settlement. The Modi government must not disregard either Vijayakant's letter or the concerns it raises.

Senior advocate Ramasamy, who appeared for the Income Tax Department, told reporters that he had not opposed the request and that the magistrate had therefore adjourned the hearing. Was he instructed by the Department not to object?

Jayalalithaa and Sasikala filed petitions seeking discharge from these cases and then delayed pursuing them, following which the Income Tax Department approached the High Court and Supreme Court. On 4 September 2006, the Supreme Court ordered the trial court to decide within four weeks the case filed by Jayalalithaa and Sasikala seeking discharge. The Chief Metropolitan Magistrate accordingly heard and dismissed Jayalalithaa's petition.

In other words, in a case filed in 1997, the accused submitted a discharge petition nine years later, and that petition too was dismissed. Could Jayalalithaa's side not have said then that they wished to resolve the matter through the Department? Did they refrain from making this request because the government at the Centre at that time would not listen to them?
After the Egmore Magistrate dismissed their discharge petition, Jayalalithaa's side appealed to the Madras High Court. The High Court dismissed that appeal too on 2 December 2006.

They then appealed to the Supreme Court against the High Court's order. After hearing the appeal, the Supreme Court said: "There is no fundamental ground for interfering with the order of the Madras High Court; there is no merit in the case sufficient to admit the appeal." On 30 January 2014, it directed the trial court to complete the trial within four months. Did Jayalalithaa's side say even then that they wished to settle the matter through the Department? They did not.

The Supreme Court judgment also stated: "The accused must establish before the trial court the circumstances in which they failed to file their income-tax returns." It must therefore be remembered that the burden of establishing their innocence in this case rests with Jayalalithaa and Sasikala.
Another income-tax case against Jayalalithaa came before Supreme Court Justices B. N. Agrawal and A. K. Mathur on 24 February 2006. Jayalalithaa's lawyer sought six weeks' time.

The judges then asked: "You are making a mockery of the administration of justice. How much longer can you drag out these court proceedings?" Could they not have said even then that they would resolve the matter through the Department? Or had they already approached the Department and been told that their request would not be accepted?

We must also not forget that Jayalalithaa invited Yashwant Sinha to her home for a meal while he was Union Finance Minister and made a representation concerning the income-tax case, as he himself recorded in his book.

It is also worth remembering that when the previous United Progressive Alliance government sought a compromise by granting Vodafone a concession under income-tax law, former Additional Solicitor General Biswajit Bhattacharyya filed a public-interest petition against it in the Supreme Court.

For eighteen years, this case has been dragged out through adjournment after adjournment, wasting the precious time of the courts and, as Supreme Court judges had already said, making a mockery of the process. The Union government and Prime Minister, and the Income Tax Department in particular, must approach this matter with caution.

If the Department accepts the request from Jayalalithaa's side after years of court time have been wasted and now agrees to resolve the case through compounding, it will set a bad precedent and encourage others who commit similar violations. Those concerned must bear this in mind when the Union government and Income Tax Department deal with the matter.
With affection,
M. K.
12 July 2014
